findings.message: SQL Injection risk
findings.why: Untrusted input flows into a SQL query via string concatenation.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?", [id])
findings.confidenceReason: Static taint path: req.body → sequelize.query.
confidenceReasons: Static taint path: req.body → sequelize.query.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: Use parameterized queries: db.query("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id=?", [id])

ruleDocumentation.title: SQL injection (taint)
ruleDocumentation.pattern: Dataflow from request/source to SQL sink without sanitization.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Attacker-controlled SQL fragments executed by the app.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Sanitizers AST may not see; verify ORMs and query builders.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Bind parameters; never interpolate user input into SQL text.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample: pool.execute("SELECT * FROM posts WHERE id = ?", [id]);

findings.message: SQL built with string concatenation or template literals can lead to SQL injection.
findings.why: Untrusted input concatenated into a SQL string can break out of the query and execute arbitrary SQL.
findings.fix: Use parameterized queries, e.g. db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]), or an ORM with parameter binding.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.sql.string-concat: Query strings assembled with user-controlled fragments.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.sql.string-concat: Query strings assembled with user-controlled fragments.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: Use parameterized queries, e.g. db.query('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?', [id]), or an ORM with parameter binding.
fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: SQL built via string concatenation
ruleDocumentation.pattern: Query strings assembled with user-controlled fragments.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Classic SQL injection leading to data exfiltration or destruction.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Constants only; ensure no dynamic segments from request data.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Parameterized queries / prepared statements.

findings.message: ORM query or write incorporates HTTP request fields without validation.
findings.why: User-controlled values in Sequelize/ORM where clauses or model creates enable injection or unsafe updates (including NoSQL-style operators when enabled).
findings.fix: Validate and normalize inputs. Use parameterized APIs and avoid passing raw req.body / req.query into where or create payloads.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.orm.request-in-query: Sequelize-style find/create/update where `where` or fields include req.* or common auth identifiers (e.g. passport-local `username`).
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.orm.request-in-query: Sequelize-style find/create/update where `where` or fields include req.* or common auth identifiers (e.g. passport-local `username`).

ruleDocumentation.title: ORM query with request-derived filters
ruleDocumentation.pattern: Sequelize-style find/create/update where `where` or fields include req.* or common auth identifiers (e.g. passport-local `username`).
ruleDocumentation.risk: Injection-style operator abuse or unintended data access when user input shapes the query object.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Heavily validated DTOs passed as variables; review key names flagged.

findings.message: Redirect target may be controlled by the user (open redirect / phishing risk).
findings.why: Passing request data into res.redirect() without an allow-list lets attackers send victims to malicious sites.
findings.fix: Validate redirect targets against a fixed allow-list or use relative paths only. Reject absolute URLs from untrusted input.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.open-redirect: res.redirect (or similar) first argument is derived from req.query / req.body without validation.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.open-redirect: res.redirect (or similar) first argument is derived from req.query / req.body without validation.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: Open redirect
ruleDocumentation.pattern: res.redirect (or similar) first argument is derived from req.query / req.body without validation.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Attackers craft links that send users to phishing or malware sites while appearing to originate from your domain.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Redirects built only from server-side allow lists.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Allow-list redirect targets or use relative paths only; reject absolute URLs from untrusted input.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample:
const next = ALLOWED.has(req.query.next) ? req.query.next : "/home";
res.redirect(next);

findings.message: Deserializing untrusted data can lead to remote code execution.
findings.why: Functions like `unserialize` execute attacker-controlled object graphs. Never deserialize raw user uploads or request bodies without strict schema validation.
findings.fix: Use JSON.parse on a strict schema, avoid `node-serialize` / `serialize` on untrusted input, or use signed payloads with verified keys only.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.deserialize.untrusted: Calls such as `unserialize` on non-literal data (uploads, buffers, request fields).
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.deserialize.untrusted: Calls such as `unserialize` on non-literal data (uploads, buffers, request fields).

findings.message: Avoid weak hash algorithm 'md5'. For password storage use a password hashing function; for general hashing use SHA-256+.
findings.why: MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken. If this hashes passwords, they are vulnerable to brute-force. For integrity/signatures use SHA-256 or SHA-3.
findings.fix: For passwords: use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or argon2 with salting (e.g. bcrypt.hashSync(password, 10)). For general hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') or crypto.createHash('sha512').
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: For passwords: use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or argon2 with salting (e.g. bcrypt.hashSync(password, 10)). For general hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') or crypto.createHash('sha512').
fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: Weak cryptographic hash
ruleDocumentation.pattern: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Fast hashes enable collision and brute-force attacks.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Non-security uses (checksums of public blobs) may be acceptable—suppress with justification.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Use SHA-256+ for integrity; use Argon2/bcrypt/scrypt for passwords.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample:
import { createHash } from 'node:crypto';
const h = createHash('sha256').update(data).digest('hex');

findings.message: Avoid weak hash algorithm 'md5'. For password storage use a password hashing function; for general hashing use SHA-256+.
findings.why: MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken. If this hashes passwords, they are vulnerable to brute-force. For integrity/signatures use SHA-256 or SHA-3.
findings.fix: For passwords: use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or argon2 with salting (e.g. bcrypt.hashSync(password, 10)). For general hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') or crypto.createHash('sha512').
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.

findings.message: Avoid weak hash algorithm 'md5'. For password storage use a password hashing function; for general hashing use SHA-256+.
findings.why: MD5 and SHA-1 are cryptographically broken. If this hashes passwords, they are vulnerable to brute-force. For integrity/signatures use SHA-256 or SHA-3.
findings.fix: For passwords: use bcrypt, scrypt, PBKDF2, or argon2 with salting (e.g. bcrypt.hashSync(password, 10)). For general hashing: crypto.createHash('sha256') or crypto.createHash('sha512').
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule crypto.hash.weak: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

ruleDocumentation.title: Weak cryptographic hash
ruleDocumentation.pattern: MD5/SHA1 via crypto.createHash, or MD5 helpers such as the `md5` npm module, used in security-sensitive workflows.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Fast hashes enable collision and brute-force attacks.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Non-security uses (checksums of public blobs) may be acceptable—suppress with justification.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Use SHA-256+ for integrity; use Argon2/bcrypt/scrypt for passwords.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample:
import { createHash } from 'node:crypto';
const h = createHash('sha256').update(data).digest('hex');

findings.message: ORM query or write incorporates HTTP request fields without validation.
findings.why: User-controlled values in Sequelize/ORM where clauses or model creates enable injection or unsafe updates (including NoSQL-style operators when enabled).

findings.message: Insecure env var fallback — if env var unset, hardcoded secret is used
findings.why: If the app runs without the env var set, a guessable default may be used in production.
findings.fix: Remove fallback. Throw error if env var is missing at startup.
findings.remediation: Remove fallback. Throw error if env var is missing at startup.

ruleDocumentation.title: Weak or default secret fallback
ruleDocumentation.pattern: Secret material falls back to a weak literal when env/config is missing.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Deployments without proper env setup silently use attacker-guessable secrets.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Test fixtures with clearly fake literals; ensure production paths use vault/env only.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Fail closed when secrets are missing; load from a secrets manager or validated env only.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample:
const secret = process.env.JWT_SECRET;
if (!secret || secret.length < 32) throw new Error("JWT_SECRET required");

findings.message: File path may be derived from user input, enabling path traversal.
findings.why: If the path comes from req.query, req.params, or similar, an attacker can use '../' to read or write outside intended directories.
findings.fix: Resolve paths against a fixed base directory and normalize, e.g. path.join(BASE_DIR, path.normalize(userPath)). Do not use user input directly as a path.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.path-traversal: Filesystem calls combine user paths without normalization/allowlists.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.path-traversal: Filesystem calls combine user paths without normalization/allowlists.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: Resolve paths against a fixed base directory and normalize, e.g. path.join(BASE_DIR, path.normalize(userPath)). Do not use user input directly as a path.
fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: Path traversal (pattern)
ruleDocumentation.pattern: Filesystem calls combine user paths without normalization/allowlists.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Read/write arbitrary files outside intended directory.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Chrooted or strictly allowlisted roots.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: resolve + startsWith root check; use path.basename for names only.

findings.message: Log message built from user input without sanitization can lead to log injection.
findings.why: Unsanitized input in logs lets attackers insert fake entries, hide actions, or impersonate others via CR/LF and control characters.
findings.fix: Sanitize log input: strip or escape carriage return and line feed. Limit log entry size. Do not pass raw user input as the log message.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.log: User data written to logs without newline/control stripping.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.log: User data written to logs without newline/control stripping.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: Sanitize log input: strip or escape carriage return and line feed. Limit log entry size. Do not pass raw user input as the log message.
fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: Log injection (pattern)
ruleDocumentation.pattern: User data written to logs without newline/control stripping.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Forged log lines, log forgery, downstream parser confusion.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Structured logging with JSON encode may already escape.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Strip CR/LF; bound length; structured logs.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample: logger.info({ user: userId, msg: message.replace(/[\r\n]/g, ' ') });

findings.message: Unsanitized HTML insertion can lead to XSS.
findings.why: Inserting user-controlled or dynamic content into innerHTML, outerHTML, or document.write allows script injection.
findings.fix: Use textContent for plain text, or a sanitizer library for HTML. Set HttpOnly on session cookies. Avoid innerHTML with user input.
findings.confidenceReason: Pattern rule injection.xss: innerHTML, document.write, or similar with dynamic HTML.
confidenceReasons: Pattern rule injection.xss: innerHTML, document.write, or similar with dynamic HTML.
findings.proofReason: No proof pipeline output for this finding (detection-only).

fixPreview.remediation_hint: Use textContent for plain text, or a sanitizer library for HTML. Set HttpOnly on session cookies. Avoid innerHTML with user input.
fixPreview.expected_effect: Re-scan should clear the pattern if the vulnerability class is fully addressed; confirm with review.

ruleDocumentation.title: DOM XSS
ruleDocumentation.pattern: innerHTML, document.write, or similar with dynamic HTML.
ruleDocumentation.risk: Session/cookie theft via injected scripts.
ruleDocumentation.falsePositives: Trusted static markup only—prefer textContent or framework escaping.
ruleDocumentation.remediation: Encode context-appropriate output; use CSP; avoid raw HTML sinks.
ruleDocumentation.secureExample: el.textContent = userName;
